title: I Trust You Will Bring Me Good News!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source pages confirm that the damaged election-code reference is `Rule No. 7`. OCR rendered the printed list bullets variously as `॥`, `©`, `=`, `1`, and `2`; these are restored as consistent bullets in the derived Tamil reading copy. Other source-visible errors include `சம்பந்தம்`, `நிரம்பியவர்களை`, `ஏற்காடு தொகுதி`, `சத்துணவு அமைப்பாளர்`, and broken word spacing. The printed salutation is restored. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: Tomorrow is 4 December, the by-election for the Yercaud Assembly constituency! Campaigning ended yesterday. Everyone who had camped in the constituency for the campaign has returned home.

Although General Secretary Professor and I could not campaign because of our health, party Treasurer, Thalapathi and younger brother M.K. Stalin visited two or three times and has now spent four days there, travelling from village to village. Deputy General Secretaries and younger brothers Duraimurugan and V.P. Duraisamy, headquarters and district secretaries, former ministers, frontline party members and members of the various wings have spent the past several weeks canvassing votes in Yercaud. Rajya Sabha member and poet Kanimozhi campaigned there for two days.

On behalf of party headquarters, an election committee was formed comprising district in-charge and younger brother S.R. Sivalingam, Member of Parliament T.M. Selvaganapathi, R. Rajendran of Panamarathupatti, Veerapandiyar's son Veerapandi A. Raja, Member of Parliament Adhi Sankar, G. Sudamani, K. Sundaram, P. Gopal and C. Tamilselvan. They too worked effectively. Younger brother Pon. Muthuramalingam, chairman of the headquarters resolutions committee, was appointed supervisor and has remained there at work.

The ruling AIADMK appointed a sixty-one-member election committee for the Yercaud by-election. Every minister camped in the constituency. The Salem District Collector acted as a principal leader of the AIADMK election committee and, because he is due to retire soon and apparently believed no one could touch him, spun about like a top working for the AIADMK. Under any government, has every minister ever emptied the Secretariat and gone off for a single Assembly by-election?

Even before nominations were filed, election rules were violated. I wrote in detail about those actions in *Murasoli* on 10 November 2013 under the title “I Invite You with Folded Hands.” Let me point out the further violations that followed:

* Only newly eligible eighteen-year-olds could be added to the supplementary electoral roll. Yet fifteen thousand bogus voters were announced as having been added, including people as old as forty with no connection to the constituency, falsely shown as residing at the home addresses of AIADMK members.
* The DMK wrote to India's Chief Election Commissioner demanding the immediate removal of the bogus voters.

* Although the DMK candidate arrived at the specified time announced by the election officer, the officer did not accept his nomination and instead received the nomination of an independent candidate.

* Despite the Election Commission's order that government vehicles must not be used during campaigning and that campaigners must not stay in government guest houses, some ministers and board chairpersons violated these restrictions.
* Because ministers were members of the election committee, they made officials working under their departments act in support of the ruling-party candidate.
* Newspapers published photographs showing the secret distribution in Vazhapadi and Karipatti of free goods supplied by the AIADMK government, including no-cost grinders, mixers and electric fans.
* On 11 November 2013, *Dinamalar* itself reported that government assistance goods such as mixers, grinders and fans had been brought into the constituency for distribution to Yercaud voters.
* On national highways in Yercaud, officials confiscated the public's money in the name of vehicle checks, but conducted no checks on the village roads used by ministers.
* When the AIADMK candidate came to file the nomination, more than forty cars flying AIADMK flags arrived in violation of election rules.

* At about noon on 13 November, every minister and AIADMK Member of Parliament and Legislative Assembly arrived in more than one hundred flag-bearing vehicles for the opening of the AIADMK party office at Ayodhyapattinam. They removed the protective fence from government-owned poramboke land and used it for parking.
* Newspapers published a photograph of a government employee, a nutritious-meal organiser, canvassing votes alongside ministers.

* Because ministers had spent many weeks in Yercaud, important files were carried there by officials for signatures. When government officers travelled there, other officials connected with their departments had to follow.
* Rule No. 7 of the election code states that there must be no complaint whatsoever that a party governing a state has used officials for election work.
* In violation of the rules, images of the Chief Minister remained throughout Salem district at Amma canteens, Amma drinking-water outlets bearing the two-leaves symbol, and group houses.
* In violation of the rules, the District Collector himself laid the foundation stone for an Omalur Taluk office costing 2.52 crore rupees.

* A government official serving as Block Development Officer visited a minister's house for consultations in violation of election rules. Another officer and two assistants accompanied him in a Tamil Nadu government vehicle.

* In Minnampalli village, ministers brought agricultural labourers by minibus, served them upma and pongal, made everyone stand in line and distributed two hundred rupees to each person. They chased away the flying squad that went to photograph it.

At the very peak of all these activities, newspapers openly reported that two thousand rupees had been distributed to every voter. This is not a general election. It is a by-election in a single constituency. Yet the ruling party made all these arrangements for it. A few days ago, I wrote about the AIADMK's repeated involvement in such conduct whenever a by-election is held, and the occasions on which the Election Commission itself censured the party.

Should we not issue a warning against this misrule and its continuing lawlessness? That is what this by-election is for. If the ruling party is allowed to win even this election, it will be encouraged to commit still more lawless acts. It will begin to believe that no one exists to oppose it. No section of society is at peace under this government.

The suffering of farmers hardly needs to be described. Power cuts have begun again. Industries are struggling. Examinations conducted by recruitment boards are riddled with confusion. Projects already devised by the DMK government are being ceremonially buried. This regime neither does anything of its own nor continues what was already done. The government's finances are worsening badly.

If any official wants to meet the Chief Minister, that official must first write down why the meeting is sought and obtain approval before being allowed to see her. Funds due under an already announced scheme are presented by the Chief Minister to the families of deceased police personnel, and newspapers receive daily reports that she distributed funds to so many people that day. Projects devised under the party government and now completed are opened by video link. Whenever any problem arises in the state, an urgent letter is immediately written to the Prime Minister, but no steps towards solving the problem are visible.

If anyone in the opposition speaks against this government or the Chief Minister, a defamation case follows immediately. Newspapers cannot publish opinions opposed to the government. If they dare to do so, cases are filed against them too.

Prices keep rising. Electricity charges, milk prices, bus fares, nothing has been spared; everything has been increased. In the Assembly, the Chief Minister thundered, “I have made this state power-surplus. Look at our achievement!” That was all it took. The very next month, power cuts began again. When the public complains, the Chief Minister writes that the Union government and the DMK have joined hands in a conspiracy.

Should all this not be taught a lesson? Tomorrow's Yercaud by-election offers such an opportunity. Fortunately, none of the other major opposition parties is contesting. Everyone knows that I wrote to them seeking their support. A few parties did not reply. Silence is said to signify consent, and members of those parties will understand that.

Therefore, in tomorrow's election, to teach Tamil Nadu's ruling party a lesson and give it an opportunity to reform, I ask you to vote for party candidate and younger brother V. Maran under the Rising Sun symbol and secure his victory. I trust that you will bring me the good news of a DMK victory in the Yercaud by-election!
With affection,
M.K.
